race.name: Around the Bay in a Day
race.tagline: 210 km around Port Phillip Bay. Ferry crossing, Beach Road, 10,000 riders, and 30 years of Melbourne cycling tradition. Australia's biggest one-day ride, and it is not even close.

vitals.date: Third or fourth Sunday of October annually
vitals.registration: Online via Bicycle Network / Race Roster. Classic 210km ~$210 AUD. Shorter distances from ~$50-$180 AUD. Bicycle Network members receive 10% discount. Early bird pricing available.
vitals.entry_fee: ~$210 AUD (Classic 210km); varies by distance. Includes jersey, medal, ferry, rest stops, on-course support.
vitals.feed_zones: Multiple fully stocked rest stops with water, food, nutrition, mechanical support, and first aid. Ferry crossing included.
vitals.cutoff_time: Generous time limit with SAG support for all distances. Riders typically finish between 7-12 hours for the Classic.
route_options: 300 km (Legend — extended loop with additional climbing)
210 km (Classic — full circumnavigation of Port Phillip Bay)
135 km (Geelong start to Albert Park via ferry)
20 km (Family Ride — Lap the Track at Albert Park)

climate.primary: Melbourne spring — mild, variable, 11-21C with possible wind and showers
climate.description: Mid-October in Melbourne delivers classic spring weather: cool mornings around 9-12C warming to 17-21C by afternoon. The city's reputation for four seasons in one day is earned — you can start in sunshine, ride through a rain squall at Geelong, and finish under clear skies on Beach Road. Wind is the primary weather variable, particularly on the exposed western shore between Werribee and Geelong where crosswinds off the bay can be relentless. The Bellarine Peninsula is typically windswept. The ferry crossing provides a 40-minute recovery break but also a temperature reset — dress in layers.
challenges: Melbourne's famously variable spring weather — four seasons in one day is not a joke
Exposed crosswinds on the western shore between Werribee and Geelong
Possibility of rain at any point — pack a lightweight rain jacket
Cool pre-dawn start temperatures (8-12C) versus afternoon warmth (18-22C)
Wind on the Bellarine Peninsula can slow progress significantly

terrain.surface: Sealed roads and shared-use paths around Port Phillip Bay through Melbourne suburbs, Geelong, and the Bellarine and Mornington Peninsulas. The route is essentially flat — approximately 300 metres of total elevation gain across 210 km. Road quality varies from excellent (Beach Road) to fair (some rural sections near Little River). The route crosses the West Gate Bridge, follows the bay coastline through Geelong and the Bellarine Peninsula to Queenscliff, takes the Searoad Ferry to Sorrento, then returns along the Mornington Peninsula and Beach Road to Albert Park.
features: Complete circumnavigation of Port Phillip Bay — 210 km of coastal riding
Queenscliff to Sorrento ferry crossing — 40-minute scenic boat ride between peninsulas
Melbourne's iconic Beach Road — the spiritual home of Australian recreational cycling
West Gate Bridge crossing — normally closed to cyclists, opened exclusively for the event
Flat terrain throughout — less than 300 m total elevation gain

fondo_rating.scoring_notes: Migrated from gravel-race-automation. Original score: 63. adventure dimension dropped, road_surface added. Score recalculated: 63.

course_description.character: A flat, coastal circumnavigation of Port Phillip Bay on sealed roads and paths, punctuated by a 40-minute ferry crossing between Queenscliff and Sorrento. The Classic route runs anti-clockwise: west from Albert Park over the West Gate Bridge, through Werribee and Little River to Geelong, along the Bellarine Peninsula to Queenscliff, across the water by ferry to Sorrento, then back along the Mornington Peninsula and Beach Road to the finish at Albert Park. The terrain is relentlessly flat — the challenge is distance, wind, and endurance rather than gradient. The scenery shifts from urban Melbourne to industrial western suburbs, open farmland, Geelong's waterfront, the coastal Bellarine Peninsula, and finally the iconic Beach Road run home.
course_description.signature_challenge: The sheer distance — 210 km of flat coastal riding with no hills to break the monotony and no shelter from the wind. The western shore between Werribee and Geelong is where Around the Bay reveals itself: in a headwind, this section is a war of attrition. The ferry provides a 40-minute reprieve, but you still have 80 km to ride on the other side.

logistics.airport: Melbourne Tullamarine Airport (MEL) — major international hub with direct flights from all Australian capitals plus nonstop services from Asia, Middle East, and North America. 25 km from Albert Park, 30-45 minutes by taxi or SkyBus to Southern Cross Station then tram.
logistics.lodging_strategy: Albert Park, St Kilda, and South Melbourne are all within cycling distance of the start and offer abundant accommodation. Hotels near Albert Park range from $150-300 AUD/night on event weekend. St Kilda has backpackers, mid-range hotels, and Airbnb options along the foreshore. The CBD is 3 km away with the full spectrum of Melbourne hotels. Book early — 10,000+ riders means nearby accommodation fills fast, particularly the Pullman Melbourne Albert Park and Mercure Melbourne Albert Park.
logistics.food: Melbourne is one of the world's great food cities. Pre-ride carb loading options are endless — Italian on Lygon Street, Asian on Victoria Street, or any of the hundreds of restaurants in the CBD and inner suburbs. Post-ride, the Albert Park event village has food trucks and the finish-line celebration. St Kilda's Acland Street and Fitzroy Street offer everything from fish and chips to fine dining within walking distance of the event precinct.

history.origin_story: Around the Bay in a Day was born on Sunday 17 October 1993, created by Bicycle Victoria (now Bicycle Network) following the success of their Great Victorian Bike Ride multi-day touring event. The concept was simple and audacious: ride around Port Phillip Bay in a single day. The inaugural event attracted 2,700 registered riders (with another 1,000 turned away due to ferry capacity), starting at 5:30 am from JL Murphy Reserve in Port Melbourne. Despite the long distance and many riders finishing in the dark, it was deemed an overwhelming success. The event grew rapidly — 8,600 riders in 2004, nearly 11,000 in 2005, over 14,000 in 2006, and a peak of more than 16,000 in 2008. What began as a cycling event became a Melbourne institution, known as 'the ride that stops Melbourne.' Multiple distance options were added over the years, a partnership with The Smith Family charity was established, and title sponsors including Bupa and United Energy brought the event to a mainstream audience far beyond the cycling community.
notable_moments: 1993: Inaugural event — 2,700 riders depart JL Murphy Reserve, Port Melbourne at 5:30 am. Another 1,000 turned away due to ferry capacity. Many finish in the dark.
2004: Over 8,600 riders participate as the event establishes itself as Australia's biggest one-day ride
2005: Nearly 11,000 riders register, raising $300,000 for The Smith Family charity
2006: Total entries exceed 14,000, raising $440,000 for charity
2008: Peak participation of over 16,000 riders — the largest single-day cycling event in Australian history
Multiple distance options introduced over the years, growing from the original 210 km to six routes (20 km to 300 km)
2024: 30th edition of the event — three decades of continuous operation as a Melbourne institution
history.reputation: Australia's biggest one-day cycling event and a Melbourne cultural institution. The ride that stops a city of five million people for a Sunday in October. Where 10,000 riders prove that cycling is not a sport, it is a community.

biased_opinion.summary: Around the Bay is not the hardest ride in Australia. It is not the hilliest, the most technical, or the most remote. It is the biggest, the most inclusive, and the most culturally embedded cycling event on the continent. Ten thousand riders circumnavigating Port Phillip Bay on a single Sunday in October, crossing the water by ferry, finishing on Beach Road as the afternoon light hits the bay — this is Australian cycling at its most democratic. The flat terrain means the challenge is pure endurance. The 210 km distance means it is not trivial. The community means you never ride alone.
strengths: Scale: 10,000-16,000 riders make this the largest single-day cycling event in the Southern Hemisphere
The ferry crossing: a 40-minute boat ride from Queenscliff to Sorrento is a genuinely unique feature no other mass-participation ride offers
Accessibility: six distance options from 20 km to 300 km welcome every ability level from children to ultra-endurance riders
Inclusions: the free jersey, medal, ferry, rest stops, and on-course support represent excellent value
Cultural weight: 30+ years of Melbourne tradition — this event has genuine institutional significance
weaknesses: Flat: 300 metres of climbing across 210 km offers zero challenge for climbers. The terrain is monotonously flat.
Non-competitive: no timing, no results, no racing. If you need a number and a podium, this is not your event.
Wind: the western shore is exposed. In a bad year, the headwind from Werribee to Geelong can be soul-destroying.
Urban sections: portions of the route pass through suburban Melbourne — not every kilometre is scenic.
biased_opinion.bottom_line: Australia's biggest ride. Not the hardest. Not the most beautiful. But the one that matters most to the most people. 210 km around a bay, across the water, and home on Beach Road. Thirty years and counting.

final_verdict.one_liner: 210 km around Port Phillip Bay with 10,000 riders and a ferry. Australia's biggest one-day cycling event, and a Melbourne institution since 1993.
final_verdict.should_you_race: Yes if you want to experience Australia's largest cycling event in one of the world's great cities. Yes if you value community over competition and want a well-supported 210 km endurance challenge on flat terrain. Skip if you need climbing, technical terrain, or a competitive racing environment.
final_verdict.alternatives: If you want an Australian cycling event with climbing: try the 3 Peaks Challenge (Alpine region, ~235 km, 4,000+ m climbing). If you want a competitive Australian sportive: try Cadel Evans Great Ocean Road Race - People's Ride. If you want a Southern Hemisphere mass-participation ride with more terrain variety: try Cape Town Cycle Tour (South Africa).

videos.title: Bicycle Network Around the Bay in a Day 2022, road cycling Melbourne
videos.description: Bicycle Network Around the Bay in a Day 2022, road cycling Melbourne.  I go solo to Sorrento and then pick up riders returning to Melbourne.  They are doing the 210 km Around the Bay, as they depart the Ferry we cycle back picking up groups when we can.  Its a big day on the bike 210km, but a great celebration of fitness and health on the bicycle.  In Melbourne, Australia.
videos.curation_reason: Recent race footage showing the full 210km course experience with ride-along perspective.

videos.title: AROUND THE BAY 2016 WITH VON (230km Ride!)
videos.description:
AROUND THE BAY 2016 - With Von (230km Ride!)

Von completed 230km in some of the toughest conditions ever encountered on the Around The Bay in a Day ride here in Victoria. Here’s some footage of the final 15kms and Von discussing how the day unfolded… and will she do it again!? 

Links:
Von’s Strava Ride: https://www.strava.com/activities/738780583


Subscribe to this channel for new video notifications!

 
♫Music By♫
●Nomyn - Daydreamer - https://youtu.be/ltHCRvfdUFI
●Facebook - https://facebook.com/NomynMusic

●Soundcloud - https://soundcloud.com/nomyn

videos.title: United Energy Around the Bay 2019 — We're Riding
videos.description: Rally your workmates, your cycling club or your riding buddies for the United Energy Around the Bay! Create a team, set your goal and raise funds to help disadvantaged Australian children succeed at school.

quotes.text: They're saying it's the windiest day in Melbourne for 5 years and they've got 50ks of head wind home. The wind is terrible and everyone's struggling, I'm even struggling to stand up.
I've never ridden this much winds down Beach Road before ever, it's borderline dangerous.
It was a Tailwind all the way, flying past the Werribee zoo and up over the Westgate Bridge, then managed to get a 100km queue really well - I was so impressed with that.

key_challenges.name: 50k headwind section home
key_challenges.description: The final 50 kilometers feature brutal headwinds. One rider described it as the windiest day in Melbourne for 5 years, with conditions so severe that people struggled to stand up. The wind along Beach Road was described as borderline dangerous.

key_challenges.name: Ferry crossing transition
key_challenges.description: At the halfway point riders board a ferry. The key challenge is staying moving while on the ferry to avoid seizing up, as riders still have another 100km to go after the ferry crossing.

terrain_notes.text: The first 100km features favorable tailwind conditions with long trains of riders forming naturally. Rolling hills are present on approach roads like Q Boulevard. The return journey includes familiar coastline roads on the left side.

race_day_tips.text: Consider riding to the start line instead of driving - it can be faster than finding parking and provides a good tailwind warmup over the Westgate Bridge.
Start eating every half hour after the first hectic hour. The first hour involves weaving through traffic, so stay hydrated but don't worry about eating until things settle down.
Stay moving while on the ferry at the halfway point to avoid seizing up, as you still have 100km remaining after the crossing.
Line up at the back of the first wave behind the Bicycle Network riders for better organization and flow.

additional_quotes.text: I decided to ride into the start line the night before - it was going to be faster than trying to find a car park getting my bike out and getting all that happening. It was really well organized and I had a tailwind all the way over the Westgate Bridge.

You've got to start with breakfast - big bowl of cereal and coffee. Stay hydrated during the half hour travel time to start, then have a little snack just before starting because you won't eat for the next hour while weaving through traffic.

rider_intel.search_text: Around the Bay Melbourne is a 137-mile ride featuring dramatically different conditions on the outbound and return legs. Riders report excellent tailwind conditions for the first 100km with natural peloton formation and steady pacing opportunities. The route includes passage over Westgate Bridge and through Melbourne suburbs before reaching rural areas. At halfway, riders board a ferry crossing where staying mobile is crucial to avoid stiffening up. The final 50km presents the race's biggest challenge with severe headwinds described as borderline dangerous conditions. Wind speeds can be extreme - one year featured the windiest conditions Melbourne had seen in five years. The return leg follows familiar coastline roads. Logistics include potential parking difficulties at the start, making riding to the start line a viable option. The first hour involves navigating through traffic and rider groups before settling into steadier rhythm and nutrition patterns.
